steps.title: Identify what counts as “before Harlem”
steps.content:
The student's goal is clear: emphasize accomplishments before moving to Harlem (1914).

The notes identify exactly two works from the pre-Harlem period — Songs of Jamaica (1912) and Constab Ballads (1912), both published while McKay lived in Jamaica.

Any choice that emphasizes these two works meets the goal; any choice that mixes in post-Harlem works like Harlem Shadows (1922) or Home to Harlem (1928) fails to emphasize the pre-Harlem period.

steps.title: Match the goal to the choices
steps.content:
Choice B — Although he is best known as a Harlem Renaissance writer, Claude McKay had published two acclaimed poetry collections in 1912 while living in Jamaica: Songs of Jamaica and Constab Ballads. directly accomplishes the goal: it names the two pre-Harlem works and explicitly positions them as early accomplishments despite McKay's greater fame as a Harlem Renaissance writer. The contrast structure ("Although he is best known...") ensures the reader understands these 1912 publications in Jamaica are noteworthy in their own right.

Choice A — Jamaican American writer Claude McKay is the author of works such as Songs of Jamaica (1912), Constab Ballads (1912), Harlem Shadows (1922), and Home to Harlem (1928). lists all four works without temporal distinction, muddling the emphasis. Choice C — In 1914, Claude McKay moved to Harlem, where he would become known as a poet and novelist of the Harlem Renaissance (a literary and cultural movement of the 1920s and 1930s). shifts focus entirely to the 1914 move and McKay's Harlem-era reputation. Choice D — Before moving to Harlem, Claude McKay—author of the poetry collection Harlem Shadows (1922) and the novel Home to Harlem (1928)—lived in Jamaica. contradicts itself — it claims to discuss the pre-Harlem period but then cites post-Harlem works like Harlem Shadows (1922) and Home to Harlem (1928), with titles that explicitly invoke Harlem.

That matches Choice B — Although he is best known as a Harlem Renaissance writer, Claude McKay had published two acclaimed poetry collections in 1912 while living in Jamaica: Songs of Jamaica and Constab Ballads..
